name: Each-uisge
description: The Each-uisge, also known as the Eachy, is a dangerous water spirit from Scottish folklore, primarily inhabiting lochs and freshwater pools. Meaning 'water horse' in Gaelic, it is a shapeshifting creature, most commonly taking the form of a handsome man or a beautiful horse to lure unsuspecting victims. Unlike the Kelpie, which primarily inhabits rivers and streams, the Each-uisge dwells in deeper, more secluded bodies of water. Its true form is said to be monstrous and part fish, with the ability to control the water around it. The Each-uisge is considered to be one of the most perilous creatures in Scottish mythology, as it is believed to kill and devour those it manages to ensnare, leaving no trace of their remains except for the lungs or liver that float to the surface. It is a predator to be avoided at all costs.
subtitle: A deadly shapeshifting water spirit of Scottish lochs.
appearance: Typically appears as a handsome man or a beautiful horse, but its true form is monstrous and part fish. Often described as having a sticky hide.
habitat: Lochs and deep freshwater pools in Scotland.
behavior: Lures victims with its attractive guise, then drags them into the water to drown and devour them. Very territorial and aggressive.

keyMyths.title: The Each-uisge of Loch Ness
keyMyths.description: Tales abound of the Each-uisge inhabiting Loch Ness, preying on travelers who venture too close to the water's edge. One story tells of a young woman who was charmed by a handsome stranger, only to be dragged beneath the surface and never seen again, her fate attributed to the malevolent Each-uisge.

keyMyths.title: The Horse of Death
keyMyths.description: Another common tale involves the Each-uisge taking the form of a magnificent horse, enticing riders to mount it. Once mounted, the horse would bolt into the loch, sticking to the rider and drowning them. The horse's skin is said to be sticky allowing no escape.

keyMyths.title: The Legend of Loch Awe
keyMyths.description: Local legend tells of the Each-uisge in Loch Awe, terrorizing the surrounding villages. Only a brave hero with a sword of iron could defeat the creature, driving it away from the loch and bringing peace to the region. This story highlights the creature's vulnerability to iron.

sources.author: Campbell, John Gregorson

sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Scottish folklore, including detailed accounts of the Each-uisge and its various forms.
sources.text: A comprehensive collection of Scottish folklore, including detailed accounts of the Each-uisge and its various forms.
